stories.headline: Gov. Polis Defends Tina Peters Commutation, Says Decision 'Will Be Remembered Fondly'
stories.summary:
Colorado Gov. Jared Polis said Tuesday he has no regrets about commuting the prison sentence of former Mesa County Clerk Tina Peters, telling an audience at The Colorado Sun's annual legislative recap event at the University of Denver that "the nation needs to have a reconciliation and healing." Peters was convicted in August 2024 of orchestrating a security breach of her county's election system and was sentenced to nine years in prison. Polis cut her sentence in half last Friday and ordered her released on parole June 1.

The governor argued that comparable public officials convicted on nearly identical charges received probation or sentences of one to two years, and said the nine-year term was influenced by the judge citing Peters' courtroom conduct and speech. "Nobody should be punished for their beliefs, you should be punished for crimes," Polis said. Critics, however, say the commutation will embolden election conspiracy theorists ahead of the 2026 midterms.

The backlash has intensified since Polis made the announcement Friday. Some Democrats have called for his impeachment or censure, and U.S. Sen. Michael Bennet, who is running for governor, said on CNN that he views the decision as "disqualifying" and would not consider appointing Polis to his Senate seat. Vice President J.D. Vance said Tuesday that Peters would be a prime candidate for a proposed $1.8 billion fund to compensate people who feel they were targeted by the Biden administration's Justice Department.

Polis pushed back against suggestions he was caving to political pressure, saying he acted because he believed the sentence was unjust. He noted that millions of Coloradans believe Peters was punished for her beliefs and said he opposed the Trump administration's effort to financially compensate her.

stories.headline: Aurora City Council Advances Ordinance Requiring ICE Detention Facility to Report Health Hazards
stories.summary:
The Aurora City Council on Monday advanced proposed changes to city code that would require all detention facilities, including the GEO Group's U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement facility in Aurora, to report health, safety, and welfare hazards to the city upon request. The proposal, introduced by Councilmember Gianina Horton, also requires the city to request health and safety information from detention facilities on a monthly basis and mandates that facilities grant police and fire personnel access for emergency responses and criminal investigations.

City Attorney Pete Schulte acknowledged that because the GEO facility is a federal facility operated by an independent contractor, legal enforceability of the new rules remains uncertain. However, he said the ordinance establishes clear expectations and gives GEO the opportunity to voluntarily comply, and that the city would be able to file a criminal charge against GEO for noncompliance — though federal preemption arguments would likely follow.

Horton said the proposal was prompted by reports from nonprofit organizations alleging potential health issues within the GEO facility that were not reported to the city. GEO did not respond to a request for comment. The move comes as Colorado lawmakers also passed House Bill 1276 this session, which expands health and safety inspection requirements for immigration detention centers statewide.

stories.headline: Colorado Revegetation Bill Adds Protections Against 'Buy-and-Dry' Farm Water Transfers
stories.summary:
A new Colorado bill targeting the "buy-and-dry" practice of cities purchasing agricultural water rights and leaving farmland barren has cleared the legislature and is headed to Gov. Jared Polis for his signature. House Bill 1340 establishes that when irrigation water is permanently removed from farmland, the entity acquiring the water is responsible for revegetating and reclaiming the affected land. The bill primarily applies to the Lower Arkansas River basin in southeastern Colorado.

The legislation also requires water courts to incorporate county revegetation criteria into change-of-use decrees, giving local governments stronger enforcement mechanisms. Jack Goble, general manager of the Lower Arkansas Valley Water Conservancy District, called it a landmark step in protecting rural communities and neighboring landowners when agricultural water is transferred to municipal uses.

Aurora Water, which has operated in the Lower Arkansas Valley for decades and has an office in Rocky Ford, said it supports the bill's intent and does not anticipate it significantly changing its current operations in the region. However, Aurora Water expressed concern about any future expansion of similar requirements to other river basins in Colorado, saying it could inadvertently harm dryland farming operations or create disincentives for farmers already operating without irrigation.

stories.headline: Six Broad Themes Defined Colorado's 2026 Legislative Session, Analysis Finds
stories.summary:
A new analysis by the Colorado Capitol News Alliance — a collaboration of The Colorado Sun, Colorado Public Radio, and KUNC — identified six major themes shaping the recently concluded 120-day legislative session. Chief among them was internal Democratic Party tension, as more moderate members of the Opportunity Caucus and more progressive lawmakers clashed over priorities including data center regulation, immigration officer accountability, and price gouging legislation, killing several high-profile bills. Democrats also split over a proposal to require nonprofit caucuses at the Capitol to disclose donors and spending, which ultimately failed.

Despite the internal friction, Democrats secured some significant wins. A major homeowners' insurance bill subsidizing hail-resistant roofs passed in the final days of the session, and lawmakers referred a TABOR refund measure to voters that would redirect money to education funding. Additional incremental progress was made on housing and gun regulations.

The session was heavily constrained by a budget deficit that left little room for new programs or spending. Bipartisan cooperation emerged on select issues, including a bill to increase overtime pay thresholds for farmworkers, which passed with Republican support despite Democratic opposition. The analysis paints a picture of a legislature navigating tight finances, a divided majority party, and a governor with an independent streak.

stories.headline: New Details Emerge in Two Separate Fatal I-25 Crashes Over the Weekend
stories.summary:
New details have emerged about two fatal crashes on Interstate 25 over the weekend that together left two people dead and multiple others injured. In the first incident on Sunday, a head-on collision in a reversible HOV lane left Berthoud Fire Lt. Ken Bradley critically injured while he was driving to work. The wrong-way driver was pronounced dead at the scene. Bradley suffered multiple fractures in both legs, a fractured left arm, a dislocated right shoulder, broken ribs, a pneumothorax, a significant leg laceration, and a concussion, according to a GoFundMe established in his name.

The second crash, which occurred Friday near Happy Canyon Road in Douglas County, involved a 31-year-old Aurora man backing his Chevrolet Equinox along the right shoulder of I-25 when it drifted into a travel lane. A northbound Audi struck the rear of his vehicle, and a third vehicle then collided with the Audi. The Equinox driver was pronounced dead at the scene. A 46-year-old Parker woman driving the Audi was taken to a hospital with serious injuries, and a 58-year-old Castle Pines woman in the third vehicle sustained moderate injuries.

The Friday crash remains under investigation by the Colorado State Patrol's Vehicular Crimes Unit. The Douglas County Coroner's Office will release the name of the deceased driver once next-of-kin notifications are completed. All three vehicles came to rest on the interstate, temporarily blocking lanes of traffic.

stories.headline: Denver-Based York Space Systems to Acquire Solar Cell Manufacturer Solestial
stories.summary:
Denver-based York Space Systems announced Tuesday it has signed an agreement to acquire Arizona-based Solestial, a company that manufactures low-cost solar cells for space applications. The deal will be paid with 2.35 million York shares plus cash and is expected to close in the second quarter of 2026, according to federal filings. York said the acquisition will bolster its solar capabilities and reduce reliance on Chinese materials by strengthening its domestic supply chain.

Solestial produces ultra-thin silicon solar cells that are hardened by radiation using what the company calls "self-healing silicon technology," which it says is the only technology of its kind proven in orbit. About 95% of Solestial's supply chain is already U.S.-based. York's chief technology officer said the technology will allow the company to build more capable satellite platforms at lower cost with greater design flexibility.

The acquisition is the latest in a series of deals by York this year as it expands aggressively. The company previously announced agreements to acquire U.K.-based satellite communications firm All.Space and closed on the acquisition of Michigan-based electric propulsion manufacturer Orbion Space Technology. York, which went public at the start of 2026, reported $116 million in first-quarter revenue — a 9% year-over-year increase — though net losses ballooned to $114 million in Q1, up 879%, largely driven by going-public costs and acquisition activity.

stories.headline: Colorado Coalition for the Homeless Demolishes La Quinta Hotel to Build 60 Affordable Housing Units
stories.summary:
The Colorado Coalition for the Homeless has begun demolishing a former La Quinta hotel in Denver to make way for a new development of 60 affordable housing units. The project on Park Avenue represents a conversion of underutilized commercial lodging property into permanent affordable housing in the city. No timeline for completion was immediately available.

The development is part of a broader push to address Denver's housing affordability crisis by converting commercial properties into residential units. The Colorado Coalition for the Homeless has been an active player in Denver's affordable housing landscape for decades, operating shelters, clinics, and permanent supportive housing across the metro area.

In a separate local business note, Good Times Restaurants has enlisted Denver advertising agency Cultivator Advertising to refresh the nearly 40-year-old burger chain's branding, including new colors, a new logo, and updated restaurant designs.

stories.headline: DIA and Frontier Airlines Rank Below National Average in New Flight Delay Study
stories.summary:
Denver International Airport posted an on-time arrival rate of 75.4% in 2025, below the national average of 76.3% for the largest U.S. airlines, according to a new study released Tuesday by consumer watchdog group CoPIRG. The "Plane Truth 2026" report found that overall U.S. airline on-time performance has reached its worst level since 2014, with nearly a quarter of flights delayed, diverted, or canceled. The findings were presented at Denver Union Station.

Denver-based Frontier Airlines ranked worst among major airlines for the fourth consecutive year in customer complaints and involuntary passenger bumpings. Frontier recorded a complaint rate of 19 per 100,000 passengers in the fourth quarter of 2025. Nationally, tarmac delays exceeding three hours increased 63% to 708 incidents in 2025, up from 435 in 2024.

One of the longest domestic tarmac delays of 2025 involved a Denver-bound United Airlines flight, where passengers at Newark, N.J., waited on the tarmac for seven hours on July 14. CoPIRG's Colorado director Danny Katz called on Congress and the Trump administration to take action to hold airlines accountable, warning travelers to expect "erratic and stressful adventures" during the upcoming summer travel season.

stories.headline: Westin Denver International Airport Completes First Phase of $25 Million Renovation
stories.summary:
The Westin Denver International Airport has completed the first phase of a $25 million renovation, reopening 37,000 square feet of updated meeting and event space. The refresh covers all 24 event spaces, including 21 meeting rooms, two ballrooms, and a 10,000-square-foot foyer and pre-function area. Design updates include floor-to-ceiling windows with mountain views, new furnishings, LED lighting, and integrated greenery.

The hotel's largest venue, the Spruce Ballroom, can accommodate up to 850 guests and anchors the conference offering alongside a network of breakout rooms used for workshops and executive meetings. Sustainability upgrades were incorporated into the renovation, including carpets made from an 80% wool blend, with additional energy efficiency and water conservation improvements planned for later phases.

The multi-phase transformation is expected to be completed by spring 2027 and will eventually include updates to guestrooms, the lobby, dining outlets, fitness facilities, and public spaces. The Westin, which marked its 10th anniversary this year, is the only full-service hotel with direct access to Jeppesen Terminal and is positioning the renovated space to attract large corporate and conference business.

stories.headline: Independence Pass to Reopen May 21 Following Completion of Annual Maintenance
stories.summary:
Independence Pass will reopen to vehicles at noon on Wednesday, May 21, according to the Colorado Department of Transportation. Crews completed annual maintenance work on the seasonal stretch of Colorado Highway 82, including replacing damaged guardrails, filling potholes, resurfacing heaved sections, trimming overgrown vegetation, removing downed trees, and clearing rockfall from the roadway and drainage systems.

CDOT Executive Director Shoshana Lew noted that below-average snowpack this season allowed crews to complete repairs earlier than in some recent years. The pass last opened on May 22, 2025, and on May 31, 2024 — making this year's opening one of the earlier ones in recent history.

Drivers are reminded that commercial and recreational vehicles 35 feet or longer remain prohibited on the pass between mile point 47.2 on the west side near Aspen and mile point 84.2 on the east side near Twin Lakes and Leadville. CDOT urged motorists to use caution on narrow sections of the roadway and remain alert to other drivers and wildlife.

stories.headline: Trey Parker and Matt Stone Call Buying Casa Bonita a 'Dumb' but Worthwhile Decision
stories.summary:
South Park creators and Colorado natives Trey Parker and Matt Stone appeared on "Jimmy Kimmel Live" this week, where they reflected on their decision to purchase and reopen the iconic Lakewood restaurant Casa Bonita. Stone said the pair spent close to $50 million on the project, which involved buying the restaurant out of bankruptcy in 2021 and spending nearly two years rehabilitating the sprawling 50,000-square-foot space before reopening it. "It was a cool thing to do, but it was a really dumb thing to do," Parker said.

Parker and Stone said that despite the financial impracticality of the investment — joking it cost them their shot at being the world's wealthiest comedians — they are glad they did it, noting no traditional business person would have taken on the project and thus it would no longer exist. Their journey to reopen the venue is documented in the streaming film "¡Casa Bonita Mi Amor!" on Paramount+.

The duo also used the Kimmel appearance to announce that the 15th anniversary run of their Broadway musical "The Book of Mormon" is returning, with both Parker and Stone expected to perform on stage. They also revealed that Season 29 of "South Park" will premiere on September 16.

stories.headline: Park Hill's Bistro Barbès to Close After 12 Years, Chef Cites Next Career Chapter
stories.summary:
Bistro Barbès, a French bistro with North African influences located at 5021 E. 28th Ave. in Denver's Park Hill neighborhood, will close permanently on June 13 after 12 years in operation. Chef and owner Jon Robbins announced Tuesday that he is moving on to the next chapter of his career. "Running Bistro Barbès has been one of the defining experiences of my life," Robbins said in a statement.

Robbins, a former Mizuna chef, opened the 30-seat restaurant in April 2014, offering French classics inflected with Mediterranean, North African, and Middle Eastern ingredients. The restaurant earned critical praise for its elegant dishes, strong wine program, and personal hospitality, and was named after the multicultural Barbès district in Paris.

Through its final weeks, Bistro Barbès is offering a four-course tasting menu for $90 per person, featuring English pea agnolotti, roasted beets, short-rib parmentier, and a cappuccino cup de crème. The restaurant continues to serve dinner Wednesday through Saturday until its last day of service.

weather.notable: About 16 degrees below the average high for this time of year.

joke.joke: I'm from Indiana. We're a proud people. Not sure what we're proud of, but we're really proud.

topStory.headline: Trump Scores Major Primary Wins, Ousts Massie in Most Expensive House Primary in History
topStory.summary:
President Donald Trump extended his grip on Republican Party politics Tuesday as voters in Kentucky, Georgia, Alabama, and Oregon held primary elections. The night's most significant result came in Kentucky's 4th Congressional District, where Rep. Thomas Massie was defeated by Trump-backed Navy SEAL veteran Ed Gallrein by a margin of roughly 10 percentage points, ending the tenure of one of the House's most vocal Trump critics.

The Kentucky race became the most expensive House primary in American history, with a total of $33 million spent on television advertising, according to ad-tracking firm AdImpact. Trump and his allies directed a substantial portion of that spending against Massie, whom the president had targeted over disagreements on issues including the release of the Jeffrey Epstein files. Trump described Gallrein as a candidate with 'a big, beautiful brain,' saying he needed only a 'warm body' to replace Massie.

Tuesday's results followed Republican Sen. Bill Cassidy's primary loss in Louisiana on Saturday, and came the same night that Georgia Secretary of State Brad Raffensperger failed to advance to a runoff in the Georgia governor's race. Raffensperger, who refused Trump's 2020 request to overturn Georgia's presidential election results, joins a growing list of Republicans whose careers have been cut short after crossing the former and current president. In the Georgia Republican Senate primary, candidates competed to align themselves as closely as possible with Trump ahead of a June 16 runoff between the top two vote-getters.

In other results Tuesday, Jim Kingston won the Republican primary in Georgia's 1st Congressional District, Houston Gaines won in Georgia's 10th District, and Christine Drazan won the Republican primary for Oregon governor. Georgia Supreme Court Justice Charles Bethel also won reelection, defeating challenger Miracle Rankin.

Despite Trump's continued dominance in Republican primaries, analysts note the contests carry different implications for the general election. Georgia, which has two Democratic U.S. senators and a competitive statewide electorate, presents a different landscape than Alabama, a reliably conservative state. Democratic Sen. Jon Ossoff of Georgia is considered a top Republican target in the fall, but Trump's approval ratings — particularly on the economy — remain below 50 percent in national polls, posing a potential liability for Republican candidates in competitive districts and states.
